speaker: interviewer
text: --two. Morris Perlmutter, January 4, 1996. You were telling a story about when your father-- [INAUDIBLE] father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- when up to the-- when I came up to the well, and I was hiding under it and all that, and I heard like my father's voice. And I thought they threw him into the well. And I looking in the well, and I didn't see him there. And I looked around. And I don't know. And I went around. And he says, help me, help me. And I-- so I heard that it comes from the [INAUDIBLE]. It's a big, huge thing. And I went on top. I rolled off the stones. And I took him out. And he was almost blue. He couldn't catch his breath. He was just hardly breathing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's one time there. Then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as we went along with the-- with the things, they always beat us up. Because, like I didn't mention-- school was not allowed for a Jew to go. And I never went to school anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: After when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after the Germans came in right away. Since then, there was no-- no school for the Jewish guys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other restrictive laws did they enact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in our town. We could-- we could walk around free. There was no-- nowhere that we couldn't go. But we had to watch, because we didn't wear no patches at that time yet. The only thing is they used to beat us up. Anyone who wanted to beat up a Jew, he was free to do so. And anyone, if you hit back or anything like that, they said, we'll kill you. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was Polish. There was Ukrainian. This town, that village, was mixed. Because it was like the Polish people moved in there, because it was a very rich district. And it was a very nice place. And so anyone, anyone a non-Jew could beat up. We even had at one time there a few Germans living. But the Germans moved out. When the Germans came in, they moved back to Germany. And they said, so-- and we continued, but not for very long. After a while, which they already almost took everything away from us-- because they came in in late September or something. I don't remember when the war-- I don't remember dates. So I can only-- we stayed over the winter. And in the spring, they took us away from there. And that was in the spring, already, of 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, before they took you away, you say that people could beat up Jews. How did the attitude of the townspeople change, the townspeople that you knew? How did that change towards the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everyone-- a Jew is no good, that's all. There was no such thing as how-- you see, the only thing is to my father, they liked my father. Like I said before, he used to go out and make some glass or anybody else. There was one time, my father-- the Ukrainians, they burned down this house. A shack burned down with the straw roof and everything. And anyway, there's a few children. And he didn't know what to do. My father said, I'll come and I'll help you. He dug up-- he, himself, dug it. He dug it and called a few of his neighbors, the Ukrainian. And they dug a-- a big ditch. And they put up some wood over it, like crossing and all that. And they made like a-- like a hut, but it's-- most of it was underground. And he put him under. He made a-- like a fireplace in there. And-- and on both sides, he used to be able to put his children. He put some furniture in there, put a little bit whatever they had, a little table. And they didn't know what to do for my father. And that was his home till he'll build another house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whoever my father-- oh, we like Gold. Gold [INAUDIBLE]. And they-- they never-- they-- they really respected. But he was still a Jew. This had nothing to do with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was still the-- the Jew. Because they used to always say that. I mean, the bakery wasn't working. The Germans never allowed to open the bakery again, nothing. So-- and they were cutting the glass. It was also secret. If anybody had a piece of glass, he'll cut it for them, so they would give him something. But others used to get all the time a bloody nose and broken up and everything else. There were a lot of-- got hurt. And this in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone make an effort to help you during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. There was no-- no one that is going to help a Jew. No way. No, no, no. We didn't have that kind of people that would help a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Germans were stationed in your town approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that town, I don't know if there were two or three or not. I've- I've never actually, to tell the truth, seen more than that-- than three. That was a station. All the Ukrainian controlled it. Because, you see, I remember they promised them their own country for the Ukrainians. They issued money for the Ukrainian in Rovno, which Rovno was the biggest city of around our place. Rovno was already cleaned out from the Jews. They killed-- that we heard already. They killed the Jews in Rovno one time. Everybody ran away and hidden, so they said they were not going to kill them anymore. And then they made the next killing. After the next killing, anybody came back to Rovno, they caught them. They took everybody back to Kostopol. Kostopol was a smaller ghetto. And they took the Jews there. We knew about that. We knew that in Rovno, they issued there money. But Jews were not allowed to carry that. That money, I might, in fact, that I have a couple of currencies from the Ukrainian-German money. But that was it. We-- we hardly managed to live. But it was not bad, because we still stayed in our own home. So whatever somebody-- a Ukrainian, a neighbor, or something like that-- got a couple of eggs or even gave a piece of-- little bit of flour, we mixed it up and made a bread or made a pancake or anything like that. So we were-- we were not starved. In our town, we were still lived through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you heard about the killing of other Jews, was there any plans? Did family talk about meeting or escaping?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was  no plan. We could not. We couldn't go anywhere else. There was nowhere to run. There were no plans. And a lot of-- which came true so, which I heard. And I never liked it, because the rabbis used to say, it's [NON-ENGLISH] times. It's [NON-ENGLISH]. It's in the name of God and everything else. And I says, and not to supposed to touch anybody else, not to lift a hand to hit. Because you make it for everybody else bad. But everybody else was dying, so it's-- yeah. And then in the spring of '4-- '42, they took everybody away from Malynsk. And they were walking, just walk. If you couldn't walk, you  were hit. We walked to a city which was like a bigger town. It's a city Berezne. And that's where they had the ghetto. They threw us in the- in the ghetto. They threw us into our room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all the people were screaming and yelling, and because we took away a little space from the others. Why'd you come here? You should have been killed. All kinds of, you know, in the ghetto, all Jews were [INAUDIBLE]. And there was no food.

speaker: interviewee
text: There we were hungry. I escaped from the ghetto. And there we had to wear the patches on both shoulders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you wear them?

speaker: interviewee
text: On both sides, on this, on the front and the back. Of course, I never wore them. I never put another-- I was so-- but I never wore a patch. Because what it it, a lot of them-- there were more Germans already there. And they used to be in practice or show the Ukrainian how to practice how to hit the patch by a gun and to kill. So I never wore. I crawled out under the wires. I came out to bring a potato for me and for my mother and for my father. My father lost his spirit as an army man and everything else. He didn't know what was going on. It was very hard in there. But what it is-- nice to bring something, you know. The first time, they didn't catch me. Then they caught me. The Jewish police caught me. They hit me. And they told me, you should wear the patches. And you should never go out there, and this and that. I never heard of it. But I went out again. Then they caught me the third time. They took me in a room. They gave me in my back, in my behind, good with a rubber hose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who caught you the third time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish-- Jewish-- Jewish police, they were hitting me. They [INAUDIBLE]. They were in the front of it. Because, you see, they were in charge. They were supposed to be. And so I couldn't sit for a long, long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were in the Jewish police? Who did they recruit? Young people? Old people?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, they were all young people. But you see, they were Berezne. I never knew in Berezne. Berezne, the only thing is I knew I had family. I met my auntie there. I met her children, her grandchildren. And all of them did not survive. One cousin, like I said, he survived. Actually, a son of his survived, too. The day of the liberation, he was shot by a Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the ghetto enclosed, or was it enclosed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, it was barbed wire surround. I crawled under the barbed wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was barbed wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was guarding it other than the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish police were guarding the inside, most of it, and the Ukrainians from the outside. They were all screaming, all the Ukrainians. The Jews had eaten all the grass around it. There was no more grass around in there. How can-- it's going to be a desert there. It's not going to be any good. But after a short period of time in there, they came and took my father away because-- to cut glass. They took him back to Malynsk. There was an army base there which the Polish built years ago. And they had to put some windows back in there, so they took my father to put the windows there. And after a few weeks later, they came. They said they taking away anybody to cut-- to dig for turf. I told you that, what it meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, peat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Peat. Yeah, OK. So we were volunteering. Because, right away, they give us a loaf of bread. So me and my mother and my brother and another few Malynska people, we were the first ones to go. And they didn't walk us. They actually had wagons, horse and wagons. And they took us on the other side of Malynsk. There was a place which was a lowland, a wetland. And we used to dig pieces of the dead peat out of it. We used to take it out. And we used to pile it on to dry it. And we stayed there. And to live there, we had a big, huge barn. More of a shack than a barn there with some straw in there. And we would lay in there.

words.text: More of a shack than a

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was not good. But it was better than in the ghetto. Because there were a lot of Ukrainians that used to come. Sometimes they used to bring a loaf of bread or something like that. And they used to-- and it was better than in the ghetto. Because it was more free than when we were on the outside in the field a little bit and all that. But we were still hungry, never enough to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainian. There were no Germans coming there. The Ukrainian, they were all dressed up and coming on the horses and everything else. And if they wanted, to beat up somebody, they just beat them up. And you couldn't breathe and all that. They were just were very, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in the barn, or working at it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that barn, I think we were about 100 people or more. I don't remember exactly. But that was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the morale like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was no good, because everybody did not know what is the endpoint. They didn't know what is tomorrow coming. And everybody was just-- just down to this. [INAUDIBLE] we were there for a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you communicate with your father during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, there was no way of communicating with anybody. So once you were out, you were out. And we stayed there for just about the middle of the summer. I don't remember what time, because the killing of the Berezne [INAUDIBLE], and all was done in one day. Because it was on a Tuesday. And Tuesday is a holy day for the Jews. And because we discussed this after in the bushes and all that. So they said they were coming to take us to be killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: They told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: There's somebody came from-- ran away from Berezne. And they came to run in. And they said, they'll come to you. Because they're cleaning out the ghetto. They'll come to take you back to Berezne, and they'll kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this person tell you what was happening in Berezne at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was telling us that they murder. I can tell you an incident that I was in ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At one time, they took a rabbi. We had a rabbi. Rabbi Gadaze was a very famous rabbi around the district. And everybody had real respect. They told him to dig a hole. He dug a hole very deep enough. And they buried him alive. They watched. They took their time, and they watched. And then they dug him up, and they brought him back to life just to see how long can a person last under the ground. And they showed to the Jews, this is for a rabbi, because you believe in him. Now, a lot of the Jews who came and told us they were going to kill us, they didn't believe. A lot of them went back to Berezne. Of course, they were killed. Me and my mother and my brother-- it was in the evening-- we snuck out of there. And we ran. We started running all night long [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] through ditches, through mud. We scratched. We ripped ourselves apart all night long. This I'll never forget. And we thought we don't know where we were, we ran away so far. At dawn, we stopped. During the daytime, we said, we were not going to run. People would see. We looked out through the bushes. And we saw we were back in the same spot where we left-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] exactly. And which, we snuck back in slowly, slowly. And we said we were going to see my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went around through the bushes and everything else. And I said to my mother, I'll go alone. And I went into the barracks of the army base.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found my father. And he said, I know they're killing. So he left with me. And we stayed in a little forest, not a big one. There were only like 20 trees, 30, or something like that, just in a small space that we can almost see almost all around if somebody's coming. We use to lay there. And we didn't know if anybody else had survived. We just were the four people, us, for a long time And the only thing was, it was still the summer. We used to dig out a few potatoes, a few carrots or some beets. And we used to chew on it. And that's how we used to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, just-- first, on the ground just like that. No, we slept just like that. But when it started getting a little bit colder, we dug a hole. And we crawled underground. Like, my father always said, it's underground that the frost would not catch you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we dug under the ground. And we used to sleep there. And we covered it up with leaves or anything like that, even somebody will just go by and not see us. But when it started getting cold one time, we said-- my father said, we'd make a little fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: We fell asleep. And that caught on fire. And we crawled out with our lives. And we ran away, because we were not going to put the fire out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the fire, underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fire was just above us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Above you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Because the fire was on the ground. But it dried up the leaves, so it caught fire. The leaves and all the branches, you know, they caught on fire on top of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we crawled out. And we ran away from there, all four of us. We didn't make any fires for a while till winter started setting in. So we snuck into a barn to sleep in the hay. And one night, we heard somebody there. We called out, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], because that was the name that anybody who was a Jew would know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they wouldn't say anything till my father got a little bit [? upset. ?] And he just went over there. He caught him. It was one of our neighbors, a Jewish man. And we said, let's stick together, and everything else. In the morning, we got up. He was gone. He-- he didn't want. He wanted to be alone. A lot of people wanted to be alone. They were afraid if there was a group, it's no good. So he ran away. So there remained four of us. And we had to leave the barn early. Because if the farmer came in or anything like that, he could kill us. Because at that time for any Jew to give away to the Germans, dead or alive, you would get 2 or 3 kilos of salt. And that was a big treasure. Salt was not available in our district. So we had to run out and hide in another [INAUDIBLE] little bush and everything else. And that's where we stayed for a long time from one farm to another, to one barn to another.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were hiding out, did you see patrols or did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Control?

speaker: interviewer
text: Patrols, or soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: No patrols. We didn't see nobody. We-- we-- if they would catch they would-- there was nobody was [? heading. ?] Because they knew that the Ukrainian would not feed us. And they would not give us-- there were no soldiers, no army. Nobody was patrolling. I'll tell you a secret what happened one time. We came out-- because we were only four. And all of a sudden, we saw somebody go. We hide. We see him coming up to the bushes. And he was in a daze. He thought he was not in this world even. And he was one of the Jewish boys. He was thick, like dried-up blood on top of him. My mother told him to take it off. He took everything else-- he was in the nude, because there were no other clothes to give him. Because we didn't have any other clothes, which we had on. She rinsed it out in the river. And the river turned red from blood. He crawled out from a grave. And he was walking around for days and days alone. But he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he tell you anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He was shot-- they were shot. And he fell in. And he-- they fell on top of him. And he crawled out. The only thing is he's not alive. He died in '48 in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other incidents do you recall from that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At a time a little bit later, we went away. We went into the-- the bigger forest. And we stayed there underground. My father left us. He couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- at first, he was walking around with us. At night, he used to stay at a crossroads. He told us, because he brought some bread to us. And we asked him, where did you get the bread? So he was at a crossroads of Ukrainian towns.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the crossroads, there was a cross. They put a towel there. They put a pot of water. And they used to bring bread there. They saw him standing there with a beard and everything else. So they thought that like Jesus or something like that. And they brought him-- they gave him bread. He did it a few times. But he went to another Ukrainian, which they knew him. And they kept him, too, because he was getting sick. It was very cold. We always used to dig a hole in the ground. And we used to stay underground. At one time which I remained with my mother alone, my mother begged me to make a little fire again. So I said, all right, I'll make a fire. To make a fire, we didn't have no matches. We used to rub one stone the other and to get a spark and to get the fire going. It takes a long time. And we made a fire. We fell asleep. The snow covered us up. We woke up in the morning, my mother was-- was blind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was blind for a few months. I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother found some other people there. And we didn't want to go with the other people. Because the other people, they were younger people. I mean, older than me, but young people. And they called themselves like a 10, [NON-ENGLISH]. They had some guns. They used to go to the Ukrainian. And they used to demand bread and everything else. So he wanted to join them. He found somewhere, which I don't know where he got it, he had a gun. But he never had a bullet to it. And he went to them. Me, I always had to watch over my mother. And when she was blind, I had to take her just like [INAUDIBLE] and just stay beside her and hold her hand. Because she would never move. She was scared, too. And when-- he used to go to them, like my brother. And at one time, the Ukrainian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reported that there were Jews in the bush. And they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wanted to get rid of the Jews from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the German Army surrounded the forest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they came. And we heard them, because they came close. And we heard them, but they didn't understand the language.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we found out they had Hungarian. The Germans and Hungarian, and they surrounded. And they were killing. They killed a lot. Because you see, from the ghettos and from the bushes, they were-- a lot of Jews ran away. But they never survived, all the time was killed. At that day they were shooting, they shot my brother in the stomach. The bullet went through from the back into the front. They-- I got a bullet in my leg. It went through. And me and my mother ran away. But I didn't know that my brother was shot even, because he wasn't with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were both shot, but you  were not together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not together. And I took my mother. The only one thing, I knew directions. How small I was, I knew directions through the bush, anywhere. And I knew how to cross over to get out from the bush. Because they surrounded it. And they had machine guns from both sides. Anybody who came out from the bush they would shoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we crawled over. And we crawled over the bush, we, my mother and I. And we stayed there for a few days, till we found my brother. When was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. I don't know. It must've been already in the fall of '43, because s my father was getting worse. And they didn't want to keep him. My father, after that, came back to the bush. And he was not good. And my father died, actually, between Christmas and New Years. I remember that was in '43. He died. And I dug a hole. And it was frozen. And I-- I couldn't, because there was no shovel. I just dug it with my hands and just to go through. The only thing is is to dig a hole in the ground when it's frozen, is when it's a pine tree, they fall down. And if we move that, the dirt is always softer. And that's-- I buried my father there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time--

speaker: interviewee
text: My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother with you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still wasn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't want to come to see-- to help me or anything like that. He-- he stayed away with another. One thing, that the 10 people, they ran away. They went over further to Russia. And I-- we never heard from them again. But then after the war, we knew that they joined the Russians partisans and all that. But they were away. And after my father died and my brother wasn't feeling well-- he was trying, because he lost a lot of blood. And he was very weak. He was not a strong boy anyway, but he was very weak. And we heard-- let's say, on the other side of the track from Malynsk-- we were on one side, them on the other side-- was a Polish town. And all the Polish people left the town. The Germans gave them a train, and they took them. And we didn't know where they took them. Because some Ukrainians was telling me that they took the Polish people to Poland, or they took them to Germany to work in the camp. But they left the-- the town. When they left the town, they left the fields with-- with food. You know, there was enough potatoes. You see, if you would do that on a Ukrainian and they catch you, they would kill you to take out. Even, we stole a potato. So we took out from one bush one. We took out from the other bush another one, not to be noticeable that we dig. But over there, we had-- we-- we went over to the other side, my mother and my brother, all three of us. Then we heard from others there was a little girl with a brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had their mother with them. And one night, she froze to death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- they remained.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to pause for a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
